properties.name: amp
properties.description: PyTorch Automatic Mixed Precision (torch.amp)
properties.example_code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
model = nn.Linear(64, 32).to(device)
opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
x = torch.randn(8, 64, device=device)
y = torch.randn(8, 32, device=device)
opt.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
with torch.autocast(device_type='cuda' if device == 'cuda' else 'cpu', dtype=torch.bfloat16):
    out = model(x)
    loss = (out - y).pow(2).mean()
loss.backward()
opt.step()

properties.name: bf16
properties.description: CUDA C++ Programming Guide -- bfloat16 floating-point format
properties.example_code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
model = nn.Linear(64, 32).to(device)
opt = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
x = torch.randn(8, 64, device=device)
y = torch.randn(8, 32, device=device)
opt.zero_grad(set_to_none=True)
with torch.autocast(device_type='cuda' if device == 'cuda' else 'cpu', dtype=torch.bfloat16):
    out = model(x)
    loss = (out - y).pow(2).mean()
loss.backward()
opt.step()

properties.name: cuda_graphs
properties.description:
CUDA Graphs capture a sequence of kernel launches and their
dependencies into a single replayable object; the overhead of
graph definition and instantiation can then be amortized over many
executions. A graph is a snapshot of a workflow including kernels,
their parameters, and their dependencies, replayed as rapidly as
possible. Best for workloads with many small kernels and fixed
launch parameters; structures with variable shapes or per-step
control flow cannot be captured directly. See the CUDA C++
Programming Guide CUDA Graphs section for the full set of capture
restrictions and the API surface.
properties.example_code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
if not torch.cuda.is_available():
    raise SystemExit('SKIP: no CUDA')
model = nn.Linear(64, 32).cuda()
static_x = torch.randn(8, 64, device='cuda')
for _ in range(3):
    out = model(static_x)
torch.cuda.synchronize()
g = torch.cuda.CUDAGraph()
with torch.cuda.graph(g):
    out = model(static_x)
g.replay()
torch.cuda.synchronize()

properties.name: fp16
properties.description: CUDA C++ Programming Guide -- half-precision (FP16) floating-point format
properties.example_code:
import torch
cc = torch.cuda.get_device_capability()
generation = {
    (7, 0): '1gen (Volta)',     (7, 5): '2gen (Turing)',
    (8, 0): '3gen (Ampere DC)', (8, 6): '3gen (Ampere)',
    (8, 9): '4gen (Ada)',       (9, 0): '4gen (Hopper)',
    (10, 0): '5gen (Blackwell DC)', (12, 0): '5gen (Blackwell GeForce)',
}.get(cc, f'unknown (CC {cc[0]}.{cc[1]})')
print(f'GPU CC={cc[0]}.{cc[1]}, tensor core {generation}')
print(f'compiled archs: {torch.cuda.get_arch_list()}')

properties.name: fp4
properties.description: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090 (FP4 + 5th-gen Tensor Cores)
properties.example_code:
import torch
cc = torch.cuda.get_device_capability()
# FP4 tensor cores live on sm_100 (Blackwell DC) and sm_120 (Blackwell GeForce).
if cc < (10, 0):
    raise SystemExit(
        f'fp4 unsupported on CC {cc[0]}.{cc[1]} '
        '(requires Blackwell sm_100 or sm_120)'
    )
# Inference-only example. Real call sites use NVIDIA Transformer Engine
# or TensorRT-LLM with an MXFP4 recipe. PyTorch native fp4 autocast
# does not exist (as of torch 2.12); MXFP4 is reached via te.fp8_autocast
# with a Format.MXFP4 recipe in recent transformer_engine releases.
print(f'GPU CC={cc[0]}.{cc[1]} supports MXFP4 inference via Transformer Engine')

properties.name: fp8
properties.description: FP8 Formats for Deep Learning (Micikevicius et al., 2022)
properties.example_code:
import torch
if not torch.cuda.is_available():
    raise SystemExit('SKIP: no CUDA')
cc = torch.cuda.get_device_capability()
# FP8 Tensor Cores require compute capability >= 8.9 (Ada / Hopper / Blackwell)
if cc < (8, 9):
    raise SystemExit(
        f'SKIP: fp8 requires CC >= 8.9, got {cc[0]}.{cc[1]}'
    )
try:
    import transformer_engine.pytorch as te
    from transformer_engine.common.recipe import DelayedScaling, Format
except ImportError:
    raise SystemExit('SKIP: transformer_engine not installed')
recipe = DelayedScaling(
    margin=0,
    fp8_format=Format.HYBRID,    # forward=E4M3, backward=E5M2
    amax_history_len=16,
    amax_compute_algo='max',
)
te_model = te.Linear(64, 32).cuda()
x = torch.randn(8, 64, device='cuda')
with te.fp8_autocast(enabled=True, fp8_recipe=recipe):
    out = te_model(x)

properties.name: int8
properties.description: PyTorch Quantization (INT8 quantized inference)
properties.example_code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.ao.quantization import quantize_dynamic
fp32_model = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(128, 256), nn.ReLU(), nn.Linear(256, 10))
int8_model = quantize_dynamic(fp32_model, {nn.Linear}, dtype=torch.qint8)
x = torch.randn(4, 128)
out = int8_model(x)

properties.name: tensor_cores
properties.description:
Set torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark=True so that when a cuDNN
convolution is called with a new set of size parameters, cuDNN runs
multiple algorithms, benchmarks them to find the fastest one, and
reuses it for the rest of the process for the same size parameters.
Disabling it (benchmark=False) makes cuDNN deterministically select
an algorithm, possibly at the cost of reduced performance. The
separate torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic flag controls
reproducibility and is distinct from the benchmark setting; combine
it with seeded RNGs (Python, NumPy, PyTorch) when you need
reproducible runs.
properties.example_code:
import torch
cc = torch.cuda.get_device_capability()
generation = {
    (7, 0): '1gen (Volta)',     (7, 5): '2gen (Turing)',
    (8, 0): '3gen (Ampere DC)', (8, 6): '3gen (Ampere)',
    (8, 9): '4gen (Ada)',       (9, 0): '4gen (Hopper)',
    (10, 0): '5gen (Blackwell DC)', (12, 0): '5gen (Blackwell GeForce)',
}.get(cc, f'unknown (CC {cc[0]}.{cc[1]})')
print(f'GPU CC={cc[0]}.{cc[1]}, tensor core {generation}')
print(f'compiled archs: {torch.cuda.get_arch_list()}')

sequence packing and KV-cache budget before longer context
treat B100 as provisional until runtime and product-sheet validation [https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/dcgm/latest/user-guide/dcgm-diagnostics.html]
confirm device name, memory, and compute capability with nvidia-smi/torch before sizing
use B200/GB200 patterns only after runtime device reports B200 or GB200
hybrid Muon+AdamW only after runtime confirms a matmul-heavy training target [https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/2.12/generated/torch.optim.Muon.html]
avoid_patterns: no production sizing from B100 metadata without official SKU sheet
no B200/GB200 memory, MIG, or sm_100 assumptions from B100 name alone
no FP4/MXFP8 default without kernel and accuracy validation
no CUDA extension target without runtime-verified compute capability
no Muon for embedding/bias/norm/head/non-2D params

no optimizer comparison against undertuned AdamW baseline

properties.name: NVIDIA B200
recommended_patterns: keep precision/grad_accum/max_seq_len/optimizer configurable
use PyTorch SDPA before hard-coded flash-attn dependency [https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/2.12/generated/torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention.html]
activation checkpointing when activation memory dominates [https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/2.12/checkpoint.html]
sequence packing and KV-cache budget before longer context
use 180GB HBM3e and 8TB/s bandwidth for single-GPU model/KV sizing [https://docs.nvidia.com/enterprise-reference-architectures/hgx-ai-factory/latest/components.html]
use NVFP4/FP4 for validated LLM inference recipes [https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/transformer-engine-releases/release-2.13/user-guide/examples/fp8_primer.html]
use MXFP8 for Blackwell FP8 training recipes on sm_100+ [https://docs.nvidia.com/deeplearning/transformer-engine/user-guide/features/low_precision_training/mxfp8/mxfp8.html]
use TensorRT-LLM for production serving with paged attention/batching [https://docs.nvidia.com/tensorrt-llm/index.html]
use HGX NVLink/NVSwitch scale-up before PCIe/IB sharding
use MIG only for isolated single-GPU tenants; max 7 instances [https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/tesla/mig-user-guide/supported-gpus.html]
target sm_100 for CUDA/CUTLASS extensions
hybrid Muon+AdamW for matmul-heavy LLM training [https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/2.12/generated/torch.optim.Muon.html]
Muon for 2D hidden weights; AdamW for embedding/bias/norm/head/non-2D params
Muon ns_steps=5 momentum=0.95 nesterov=True as initial setting
Muon adjust_lr_fn='match_rms_adamw' when reusing AdamW LR schedule
custom CUDA kernels: budget 227KB block shared memory and 64 warps/SM [https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/blackwell-tuning-guide/index.html]
custom CUDA kernels: cluster size 16 only for B200-only opt-in path [https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/blackwell-tuning-guide/index.html]
evaluate MLOPart/MPS only for latency-sensitive B200 serving A/B tests [https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/boost-gpu-memory-performance-with-no-code-changes-using-nvidia-cuda-mps/]
use nvCOMP hardware decompression for Snappy/LZ4/Deflate input pipelines [https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/speeding-up-data-decompression-with-nvcomp-and-the-nvidia-blackwell-decompression-engine/]
avoid_patterns: no 192GB sizing on current DGX/HGX B200 deployments
no MIG for jobs needing full-GPU collectives
no topology-blind NCCL plan across NVSwitch/IB boundary
no FP4 default without accuracy validation
no CUDA extension missing sm_100 target
no Muon for embedding/bias/norm/head/non-2D params
no AdamW LR copied to Muon without adjust_lr_fn or retuning
no optimizer comparison against undertuned AdamW baseline
no cluster size 16 in portable CUDA kernels
no MLOPart for strict tenant isolation; use MIG instead
no decompression-engine dependency without allocation-type check and nvCOMP fallback
